questions.text: I feel most alive when experiencing something new, challenging, or intense.
I take on new projects or initiatives with excitement but sometimes move to the next thing before finishing.
I'm drawn to experiences and challenges that are edgy or push my boundaries.
When my work or life becomes too routine or predictable, I feel restless.
I find comfort and satisfaction in familiar routines, established traditions, or things I know well.
When stressed by work or family demands, I reach for food, entertainment, or other pleasures.
I sometimes overindulge (eating, drinking, shopping, screen time) even when I know I should moderate.
I'm intentional about how I spend resources on comfort and leisure activities.
When I get genuinely excited about an opportunity, I dive in with full commitment.
I'm more energized by periods of high intensity and challenge than by sustained, steady-paced work.
Boredom is one of the hardest states for me to tolerate.
Extended periods of routine or predictability wear on my energy over time.
I notice beauty, aesthetics, and sensory details more than most people around me.
I'm drawn toward professional or personal pursuits that carry an element of risk or uncertainty.
I see humor in difficult situations and use it to navigate challenges.
I can laugh and find levity in hard things without making others feel dismissed.
People would say I'm naturally lighthearted, fun, and bring levity to work or social situations.
Humor is one of my tools for processing difficult things without avoiding them.
I'm aware of how I'm perceived in professional and social circles and it affects my presentation.
I feel a genuine lift when I receive recognition from colleagues, peers, or respected community members.
When I accomplish something significant, I want people in my circles to know about it.
My sense of how I'm doing is meaningfully affected by feedback from people I respect.
I want acknowledgment from colleagues and peers when I've contributed something meaningful.
When my work or leadership goes unrecognized by people I respect, it affects me.
When someone close to me seems disappointed in a choice or action I've taken, I replay it looking for what I missed.
I sometimes say yes to people I care about even when the honest answer should be no.
I measure my career and life progress against specific peers or people at my life stage.
When someone in my network or field succeeds, I notice my internal response before I notice genuine happiness for them.
I tell people what I honestly see even if it might create friction or risk the relationship.
I speak up in professional or personal settings even when it creates tension with people who matter.
I feel genuinely energized by being part of a team, organization, or larger community.
I invest significantly in the health and cohesion of groups or organizations I'm part of.
When I feel rejected or excluded by someone professionally or personally important, it affects my mood and functioning.
When I sense a valued colleague or person pulling away, I work to understand and re-establish connection.
I feel a pull toward achievement and productivity even during vacations or periods meant for rest.
I take genuine satisfaction in completing significant projects and reaching major milestones.
When I'm not making progress toward something that matters, I feel dissatisfied and internally restless.
I think regularly about what I'm building or creating that will have impact years from now.
I'm motivated to invest in people and initiatives that will have significance beyond my direct involvement.
I measure my career's significance by its lasting impact and influence, not just immediate results.
I'm drawn to challenges large enough to matter at a scope beyond my immediate organization or circle.
You lead a major initiative to success. The organization celebrates the team, but your specific contribution isn't highlighted. Your honest feeling:

options.text: The initiative succeeded and the team was recognized — that's what matters.
Mild wistfulness about not being named, but it passes.
Internal frustration about the credit distribution that I manage.
Frustration that affects how I experience the success.

questions.text: You invested significantly in developing a leader. They achieve prominence without acknowledging your role. Your response:

options.text: Genuine pride — that was exactly what the investment was for.
Mild disappointment that moves past quickly.
A sense of loss I carry that I think is disproportionate.
Significant hurt — feels like they didn't acknowledge what mattered.

questions.text: You led a significant initiative that fails publicly. You were responsible for key decisions. Afterward:

options.text: I own it fully and directly, then focus on lessons and moving forward.
I own it publicly but privately revisit what others could have handled differently.
I take ownership but find the public nature of the failure genuinely difficult.
I struggle to separate my assessment of the failure from my assessment of my own capability.

questions.text: Someone you believe is less qualified is placed in a leadership role above you. You're convinced it's the wrong choice. Over the following months:

options.text: I commit to making them successful — the organization's success is what matters.
I perform my role well while privately holding my assessment of the decision.
My engagement is genuinely affected, though I manage my behavior professionally.
I look seriously for other opportunities — I can't sustain full contribution in this structure.

questions.text: I'm aware of how my accomplishments and standing compare to accomplished peers.
The achievements of people I respect in my field motivate me to maintain or raise my own performance.
I'm energized by environments where excellence is recognized and accomplishments are acknowledged.
I feel most effective and most like myself when I'm shaping strategy and direction.
I'm drawn to roles where significant outcomes depend on my ability to lead and influence people.
It's difficult for me to fully engage without being in a position to influence direction and vision.
I find it hard to let work go that I know could be more refined, even when further improvement won't be visible.
I'd rather develop deep expertise and mastery in key areas than be broadly competent across many.
I hold myself and my contributions to standards that most would consider quite rigorous.
